coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of physico-mechanical properties in NHDF and HeLa cell with treatment of graphene quantum dots using atomic force microscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jeon, Seong-Beom
Yi, Se Won
Samal, Monica
Park, Keun-Hong
Yun, Kyusik
coredata.dc:description: We investigated the biocompatibility of GQDs in terms of the cellular response, an aspect often overlooked. Herein, we synthesized two types of GQDs – Glu-GQDs (GQDs which are derived from glucose) and Gr-GQDs (GQDs which are derived from graphite) – with different functional groups on their surfaces. Both types of GQDs shared similar morphological features (shape and size distribution); the size distribution varied between 1.5 nm to 9.5 nm in both cases. Spectral analysis confirmed the difference in their chemical composition. The presence of nitrogen and chlorine in the Glu-GQDs is the major distinction between the two types of GQDs. Fluorescence emission of the obtained GQDs was observed at 480 nm for the Glu-GQDs, and at 550 nm for the Gr-GQDs. The cytotoxicity in NHDF and HeLa cell line was evaluated by a CCK-8 assay, and it confirmed that the cell viability was above 80% despite the high concentration (1024 μg/mL) in both cases. Cellular response after GQDs treatment was different from the control, but it was not lethal in the cell viability aspect. Furthermore, the potential of the GQDs as bio-imaging agents was examined using a fluorescence microscope and a laser scanning confocal microscope. The Glu-GQDs dispersed throughout the cells in NHDF and HeLa cell line, while the Gr-GQDs dispersed in the cytoplasm of the NHDF cells, and were distributed throughout the cell in HeLa. This study demonstrates that GQDs have potential in biomedical applications, even though their functionalities may be different.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Cytotoxicity
Interface
Cellular response
Cellular distribution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs
Cell imaging by bio-AFM and SEM
Synthesis and characterization of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIP) (No. 2017R1A2B4004700 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image and size distribution of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs. The scale bars, 50 nm (left), 10 nm (middle). Lattice spacing of graphene sheets along (100) and spacing between GQDs layer (002) are shown in figure. Size distribution with statistical calculation, n = 200. Size distribution suggest 4.575 nm, 5.138 nm of average lateral sizes of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs, respectively.

The results of XPS show a clear difference of functional groups on GQDs surface. The full scan of (A) Glu-GQDs and (C) Gr-GQDs shows that the presence of N and Cl elements is evident in Glu-GQDs. The spectrum of C1s in (B) Glu-GQDs and (D) Gr-GQDs can be categorized into 4 groups and 3 groups, respectively.

Typical Fluorescence spectra of obtained (A) Glu-GQDs and (B) Gr-GQDs (Inset: under visible (left) and under 365 nm UV radiation (right)). A legend that matches the color of the fluorescence emission graph is shown on the right-hand side of the figure. The wavelength of a legend denotes an excitation wavelength. The emission peak of Gr-GQDs shift to the right relative to the emission peak of Glu-GQDs. These result come from the different functional groups on two type GQDs.

Toxicity of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs in (A) NHDF and (B) HeLa cell line. Cell viability of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs measured using CCK-8 assay. Experiments were repeated thrice with triplicate, and significant difference between control and test samples are marked with *(p < 0.05), **(p < 0.01), and ***(p < 0.001). ROS generation level of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs in (C) NHDF and (D) HeLa cell line. The level of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs measured using DCFH-DA assay. Experiments were repeated thrice with triplicate, and significant difference between control and test samples are satisfied in all test concentration.

AFM image of cells after treatment of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs. AFM 3D images of control cells; (A) NHDF, (D) HeLa. In the case of NHDF, compared to the control group, the cell surface became much rougher when the cells were treated with (B) Glu-GQDs and (C) Gr-GQDs. The pores and blebs, which indicate cell apoptosis, were found to be larger upon cell treatment with Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs, as compared to the control group. In the case of HeLa, no specific change in cell shape was observed, but the blebs on the surface were more obvious after treatment with (E) Glu-GQDs than after treatment with (F) Gr-GQDs.

Quantitative analysis of cellular responses upon treatment with two type GQDs in NDHF and HeLa cells. Graphs show (A), (E) average cells height, (B), (F) RMS roughness, (C), (G) cell spreading area and (D), (H) stiffness of cells, respectively. Experiments were repeated thrice with triplicate and significant differences between control and test samples are marked with *p < 0.05, ** < 0.01, and *** < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of physico-mechanical properties in NHDF and HeLa cell with treatment of graphene quantum dots using atomic force microscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bio-Nanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Science, College of Life Science, Cha University, Seongnam 13488, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Science and Engineering, North Carolina State University, Raleigh 27695, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigated the biocompatibility of GQDs in terms of the cellular response, an aspect often overlooked. Herein, we synthesized two types of GQDs – Glu-GQDs (GQDs which are derived from glucose) and Gr-GQDs (GQDs which are derived from graphite) – with different functional groups on their surfaces. Both types of GQDs shared similar morphological features (shape and size distribution); the size distribution varied between 1.5 nm to 9.5 nm in both cases. Spectral analysis confirmed the difference in their chemical composition. The presence of nitrogen and chlorine in the Glu-GQDs is the major distinction between the two types of GQDs. Fluorescence emission of the obtained GQDs was observed at 480 nm for the Glu-GQDs, and at 550 nm for the Gr-GQDs. The cytotoxicity in NHDF and HeLa cell line was evaluated by a CCK-8 assay, and it confirmed that the cell viability was above 80% despite the high concentration (1024 μg/mL) in both cases. Cellular response after GQDs treatment was different from the control, but it was not lethal in the cell viability aspect. Furthermore, the potential of the GQDs as bio-imaging agents was examined using a fluorescence microscope and a laser scanning confocal microscope. The Glu-GQDs dispersed throughout the cells in NHDF and HeLa cell line, while the Gr-GQDs dispersed in the cytoplasm of the NHDF cells, and were distributed throughout the cell in HeLa. This study demonstrates that GQDs have potential in biomedical applications, even though their functionalities may be different.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of fluorescence materials composed of a single carbon layer, and have been used in biomedical research of their good biocompatibility and excellent fluorescence properties. GQDs exhibit different fluorescence properties depending on their surface state, such as functional groups, edges, defects, etc. [1–7]. Furthermore, compared to the inorganic quantum dots (CdTe, CdSe, InGaAs, etc.) [8–11], GQDs have many advantages, which include a facile preparation route, easy chemical modification, and a minimal risk of accumulation in vivo and in vitro [12]. Thus, GQDs have been investigated for various applications including bio-imaging [13], cancer therapy [14], drug delivery [15], gene delivery [16], photodynamic therapy [17], and sensors [18].

Nevertheless, the toxicity related with nanomaterials such as GQDs limits their application in biomedical fields. Nanomaterials can have an effect on cellular response, particularly the change of cell morphology and physico-mechanical properties – biophysical (Average cell height, cell spreading area, surface roughness) and biomechanical (cell stiffness) property. From now on, it is time for investigation of the toxicity and physico-mechanical properties of cells with treatment of GQDs.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is counted as a useful tool for observing cellular response when cells are treated with nanomaterials [19,20]. AFM can provides outstanding topographical information on cell surfaces. Several studies have used AFM to investigate the morphological changes induced by various treatment, such as antibacterial activity of glucosamine-functionalized gold nanoparticles on E. coli [21] and various tumor cells membranes following treatment with anti-cancer drugs [22]. Cellular response can be used as a biomarker for biomedical application such as stem cell differentiation. For example, in the case of the preosteoblasts, a change in the cell stiffness and roughness can be an index of osteogenesis [23]. In spite of this understanding, there is not enough research about these basic parameters.

Thus, in this work, the cellular response upon treatment with GQDs having different functional groups is investigated. The GQDs are synthesized by two different methods, and the chemical compositions of the Glu-GQDs and the Gr-GQDs are compared. In addition, we compare the difference in the fluorescence properties of the two GQDs. The obtained GQDs were treated to Normal Human Dermal Fibroblast (NHDF) and HeLa cells, and the cytotoxicity of the two GQDs is compared. Then, the cell morphology, and the change in its biophysical and biomechanical properties are investigated. Furthermore, we carried out bio-imaging studies and analyzed the distribution of GQDs in NHDF and HeLa cells. These results will provide valuable information at the cellular level for the biomedical application of GQDs with different functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A bottom-up approach was used to synthesize Glu-GQDs by modifying a previous study [24]. This reaction is a combination of carbonization and dehydration reactions. A top-down approach was adopted to synthesize Gr-GQDs by modifying a previous study in which hydrothermal cutting was employed [25]. The experimental details including synthesis and purification are described in Supplementary methods. Spectral analysis was performed using a UV–vis spectrometer (Jasco v-730), a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Jasco FT-IR 4600), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (PHI 5000, Versa probe, ULVAC PHI), Raman spectrometer (Renishaw). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse). The morphology of GQDs was observed via transmission electron microscope (TEM). A detailed process of characterization is described in Supplementary methods.

NHDF and HeLa cells were cultured with Dulbecco’s modified Eagles’medium (DMEM), which included 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and antibiotics (100 U/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin), with high and low glucose, respectively. The cytotoxicity was evaluated using the Cell Counting Kot-8 assay (CCK-8 assay, Dojindo, CK-04), following the manufacturer’s manual. NHDF and HeLa cells were seeded in 96 well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well and incubated for 24 h. After cell adhesion, the sample solutions were treated by serial dilution from 1024 μg/mL to 32 μg/mL. Again, they were incubated for 24 h, after which the samples were washed thrice with PBS to remove any residues. Then, 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for 1 h. A spectrometer (Synergy H1, Biotek) was utilized to measure the absorbance at 450 nm. This study was repeated 3 times to check the accuracy and reproducibility with triplicate.

ROS generation was evaluated by a DCFH-DA assay according to a previous study [26]. NHDF and HeLa cells were cultured in black 96 well plates and the concentration of cell was 104 cells/well. After cell adhesion to the well bottom, 100 μM DCFH-DA solution was introduced into each well, and the plates were incubated for 30 min. After removving the DCFH-DA solution, 200 μL of the sample solution (1024, 512, 256, 128, 64, 32 μg/mL) was added to each well. The plate was wrapped in aluminum foil to block light and was incubated for 24 h. A fluorescence spectrometer (Synergy H1, Biotek) was used to measure the intensity of the ROS probe. The excitation and absorbance wavelength was 485 nm and 538 nm, respectively, with a cut-off peak at 530 nm. Theoretically, the level of control for ROS generation was assumed to be 0, and the relative ROS generation level of cells treated with samples of different concentrations was estimated. The results are presented as the average of ROS intensity at 24 h subtracted 0 min from 3 times repeat of experiment with triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging by bio-AFM and SEM
ce:section.ce:para: For preparing the AFM samples, NHDF and HeLa cells were cultured on round glass coverslips with 0.2% gelatin coating, and placed at the bottom of 12 well plates. After cell adhesion on the coverslips, sample solutions (512 μg/mL) were introduced into the wells, and the plates were incubated for 24 h. Later, the cell + sample solutions were washed thrice with a 10% PBS solution. Live cell imaging was performed in liquid contact mode using a silicon nitride AFM probe (DNP, Bruker AFM probe). Images of the whole cell, nuclei, and cytoplasm were taken, and the scanning range was set to 100 μm × 100 μm, 40 μm × 40 μm, and 10 μm × 10 μm. For SEM imaging, the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. Subsequently, they were washed with serially diluted ethanol as following this order; 60%, 70%, 80%, and 90%, 99%. After cell dehydration, the samples were coated with platinum, and the SEM images were taken in the same scanning regions as in AFM imaging.

The biophysical and biomechanical properties were analyzed, according to a previous study [27]. Cells were cultured in the manner described in cell imaging by bio-AFM section. The change in the biophysical and biomechanical properties was observed using a Bio-AFM. A CONT-S sphere probe (radius: 5 μm; Nano World Services GmbH) with a 0.4 N/m force constant was used to measure cell stiffness, which indicates the Young’s modulus. Briefly, the region of interest was scanned at first, and then the cell stiffness was measured at 25 spots on the grid. The ramp size and the loading speed were set to 1 μm and 1 μm/s, respectively. Indentation forces (0.5–10 nN) were tested serially to calibrate the indentation depth in the range of 50–500 nm; this testing was performed to compensate for any defects on the cell surface and the Hertz model limitation. The tip-sample separation curves and Young’s modulus were evaluated by Hertz’s contact model with JPK data processing software (JPK instrument). The Poisson’s ratio was adjusted to 0.5. In addition, this software was used to calculate the cell height and RMS roughness. For cell spreading area analysis, the Image J software was used.

The bio-imaging study was carried out with a fluorescence microscope (IrisTM Digital Cell Imaging System, Logos Biosystems). NHDF and HeLa cells were treated with GQD solutions (512 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 2, 6, 12, and 24 h. After incubation, the cells were washed thrice with 10% PBS to remove extra GQDs, and the images were captured under visible light and GFP (excitation 470 nm, emission: 530 nm) LED filter cubes. A cellular distribution study was carried out with a laser scanning confocal microscope (Nikone Eclipse C1si & TE2000-E). NHDF and HeLa cells were cultured on 18 mm glass coverslips coated with 0.2% gelatin. After cell adhesion on the coverslips, the cells were treated with GQD solutions (512 μg/ml) and incubated for 2, 6, 12, and 24 h at 37 °C and for 6 h at 4 °C. After washing with 10% PBS to remove residual GQDs, the coverslips were mounted on a slide glass, and the confocal images were taken under a 488 nm excitation and 530 nm emission filter.

The quantitative data were analyzed to calculate the average and the standard deviation. An unpaired student’s t-test was used to investigate the variation between the control groups and the experimental groups. For all quantitative analyses, the probability of type-I error ≤0.05 was considered statistically significant. The experiments were performed thrice with triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs were synthesized successfully using the bottom-up and top-down approaches, respectively. There is no morphological difference between the two GQDs as seen in transimission electron microscopy (TEM) results in Fig. 1 . The HR-TEM images of both GQDs indicate a crystalline structure. Both GQDs have 2 lattice spacing; one (0.238 nm – Glu-GQDs, 0.235 nm – Gr-GQDs) is the lattice spacing of the graphene sheets along (100) facet, and the other (0.383 nm – Glu-GQDs, 0.385 nm – Gr-GQDs) is the spacing between the GQDs layers along the (002) facet [28]. The sizes of Glu-GQDs (Fig. 1(C)) were distributed in the range of 2–8 nm, with an average size of 4.575 nm, and the sizes of Gr-GQDs (Fig. 1(F)) were distributed in the range of 1.5–9.5 nm with an average of 5.138 nm.

Spectral analysis obviously shows the difference in the chemical bonding between the two samples. The results of the UV–vis spectroscopy can be described by following the absorption peak and the decrease band. (Fig. S1(A)) A sharp peak around 230 nm was seen in both the GQDs, which indicates a π-π* transition in the sp2 domain of the aromatic carbon. Glu-GQDs have a broad peak at 380 nm which is indicative of an n-π* transition of CO [29]. The broad peak at 380 nm appeared due to trapping of excited energy of the surface states leading to strong emission at 400 nm excitation [30,31] (Fig. 3(A)).

The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs are significantly different in the entire wavenumber range considered (Fig. S1(B)). In the case of the Glu-GQDs, the following peaks are observed: the vibration peak of CCl at 801 cm−1 reveals the presence of the Cl element derived from hydrochloric acid, on the surface of the Glu-GQDs [24]. The peak at 1070 cm−1 is ascribed to the COC bond of the epoxy group [32]. The peak at 1220 cm−1 is due to the symmetric vibration of the CNHC linkage, which indicates the presence of nitrogen on Glu-GQDs. The CO vibrational absorption peak conjugated with condensed aromatic carbons at 1628 cm−1. The CCCl/CCO peak was observed at 1743 cm−1, indicating the doping of Cl into Glu-GQDs and an unsaturated graphene CC structure in the carbonization process [24]. The CH symmetric and asymmetric stretching were observed at 2853 cm−1 and 2982 cm−1, respectively [33]. The broad peak at 3400 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of COH [24]. In the case of Gr-GQDs, the peak at 1630 cm−1 is ascribed to CO vibrations, suggesting that the carboxylic group conjugated with condensed aromatic carbons. Moreover, the stretching vibration of COH was observed as a broad peak at 3400 cm−1. These data clearly prove that the Glu-GQDs have more functional groups than the Gr-GQDs.

The Raman spectra of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs are shown in Fig. S1(C). There are two prominent bands, a broad D band at 1380 cm−1 and a weak G band at 1580 cm−1, in both the spectra. Generally, it is described that the D band is due to disordered structure of graphene sheets, while the G band is related with a primary in-plane vibrational mode. The intensity ratio (ID/IG), which is often used to correlate the crystalline purity, was evaluated to compare the two samples. The ratios of intensity (ID/IG) of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs are 1.76 and 1.3, respectively. The system with lower intensity has a lower number of defects in the functionalized graphene sheet [34]. The results of the Raman analysis elucidate that there are more defects on the Glu-GQDs containing more number of functional groups than on the Gr-GQDs.

The results of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicate surface functional groups and chemical compositions, which are well in agreement with the FT-IR data. Firstly, when the full scans area compared, the presence of N and Cl is evident in Glu-GQDs (Fig. 2
(A)–(C)). Three distinct orbital peaks were observed at ∼285 eV (C1s), ∼399 eV (N1s), ∼531 eV (O1s) in Glu-GQDs. However, there are only two distinct 1s orbital peaks in Gr-GQDs, which were observed at ∼285 eV (C1s) and ∼532 eV (O1s). The differences between the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs can be observed more clearly in a narrow scan. In the case of Glu-GQDs, the presence of N and Cl peaks can be clearly observed, whereas there is only noise at these peak values in the Gr-GQD spectrum. The spectrum of C1s in Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs can be categorized into 4 groups and 3 groups, respectively. In Glu-GQDs, the C1s XPS spectrum can be subdivided into four peaks at 284.64 eV (CC, CC, CH), 285.9 eV (COC, COH, CCl), 287.7 eV (CO, NCO), and 288.93 eV (COOH) (Fig. 2(B)). The N1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into 2 peaks centered at 399.48 eV (NH2) and 401.19 eV (NCO) respectively. The Cl2p XPS spectrum can be subdivided into 2 peaks centered at 197.33 eV (Cl2p3/2) and 198.97 eV (Cl2p1/2) derived from the spin-orbit splitting of the Cl2p core level (1.6 eV) (Fig. S2). In Gr-GQDs, the C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(D)) can be divided into 3 peaks at 284.61 eV (CC, CC), 285.94 eV (COC, COH) and 288.26 eV (CO). The relative contents of element and the bond type in Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs are listed in Tables S1 and S2. These results can describe the different functional groups in the two different types of GQDs [24,35].

In fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 3), Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs showed a blue emission and a yellow emission, respectively, under a 365 nm wavelength UV radiation. The fluorescence spectrum of Glu-GQDs showed no peak shift when the excitation wavelength shifted from 260 nm to 400 nm at 20 nm intervals. However, there was an increase of the peak of the emission wavelength at intervals of 8–19 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 420 nm to 540 nm. In case of the Gr-GQDs, there was no shift in the peak when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 480 nm at interval of 20 nm, and there was an increase of the peak of the emission wavelength at intervals of 12–21 nm when there was an increase in the excitation wavelength from 500 nm to 600 nm. This difference in the fluorescence characteristics can be attributed to the different functional groups in the two types of GQDs [24].

Cytotoxicity was evaluated in terms of the cell viability using a cell counding kit assay (CCK-8 assay). The change in cell viaiblitly at different concentrations after treatment with GQDs for 24 h can be seen in Fig. 4 (A) and (B). Compared with the control group, a statistically valid reduction (p ≤ 0.05) in cell viability was visible from 128 μg/mL in all experimental groups. In addition, the cytotoxicity depends on the concentration of the GQDs. Compared to the Gr-GQDs, Glu-GQDs show higher biocompatibility in both cells lines. Since the cell viability was 80% or more at all concentrations of GQDs, it is understood that both Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs are well suited for use in biomedical applications. Previous studies also have highlighted and emphasized the low cytotoxicity of GQDs. Chong et al. described that GQDs apparently do not affect mice, but graphene oxide (GO) exhibits toxicity and results in the death of mice [36]. In addition, Wu et al. demonstrated that GQDs show a lower cytotoxicity than GO in human gastric cancer and breast cancer cells, by evaluating the cell viability [37]. In accordance with previous studies, the two types of GQDs studied in the current work also exhibit good biocompatibility owing to their extremely small size.

To confirm ROS generation upon treatment with Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs in the NHDF and HeLa cells, a DCFH-DA assay was carried out and the levels of ROS generation were measured. It is evident from the results obviously that reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated in both NHDF and HeLa cells after they were treated with Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs for 24 h. As shown in Fig. 4(C) and (D), a higher ROS level generation level was observed in the HeLa cells than in the NHDF cells. In addition, Gr-GQDs generated higher ROS levels than the Glu-GQDs, and both GQDs showed dose-dependent ROS generation. Previous studies demonstrated that some metal nanoparticles result in ROS generation because of their chemical composition and surface states [19,37]. Thus, we surmise that the functional groups and the surface states of the GQDs are possibly involved in the ROS generation [38]. A careful study of the results may reveal the relationship between ROS levels and different functional groups.

Bio-imaging was carried out using a fluorescence microscope to capture the fluorescence images at the cell surface. Images were taken after the cells were incubated for different time periods (2, 6, 12, and 24 h). The green color was derived from the fluorescence of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs; this fluorescence emission is restricted to green owing to the filter cube (GFP, Excitation: 470 nm, Emission: 530 nm). The images are presented in terms of the fluorescence, bright field, and overlay image. The scale bar of the yellow line is unified to 100 μm. In the bio-imaging analysis, the pattern of fluorescence emission at the cell surface after GQD treatment was similar for Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs. Fluorescence emission was seen in both cases, except for the nucleus in NHDF. The intensity of the fluorescence emission after cell treatment with Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs increased with increasing incubation time, but this increase was restricted to a 6 h incubation time in the case of NHDF cells (Fig. S4). On the other hand, in HeLa, the intensity of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs increased with an increasing incubation time until 24 h (Fig. S5). Cellular distribution was analyzed using laser scanning confocal microscope; the cellular internalization of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs was examined. The images were converted into 3D-reconstructed confocal images, where the scale of the X and Y-axes was 100 μm and a yellow line indicates the cross-section. The fluorescence intensity of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs increased with incubation time, which indicates that the cellular uptake of the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs is time-dependent. Fluorescence emission was not observed in the control group. In NHDF (Fig. S6), the pattern of fluorescence emission between the Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs is different. Glu-GQDs were distributed throughout the cells without any distinction between the nucleus and cytoplasmic region, whereas Gr-GQDs were distributed in only the cytoplasmic region. In HeLa cells (Fig. S7), Glu-GQDs were distributed throughout the cells. In order to investigate the mechanism of cellular uptake of the GQDs, laser scanning confocal microscopy was utilized. The fluorescence of both Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs was observed from two cells incubated with GQD at 4 °C, as shown in the bottom of Figs. S6 and S7. The endocytosis of cells generally is blocked at a low temperature. These images imply that the cellular uptake pathway of GQDs in NHDF and HeLa cells is through a direct cell membrane penetration, rather than an energy-dependent pathway such as endocytosis. Previous studies demonstrate carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, etc directly penetrate the cell membranes [42–45]. These results suggest that GQDs can be used as good bio-imaging agents regardless of their functional groups.

Atomic force microscopy has been utilized as a powerful tool for morphological and cytomechanical properties of cells. These properties are considered as crucial factors for biomedical applications. In order to apply nanomaterials in biomedical fields, it is important to measure and quantify the cellular response to the treatment of nanomaterials. Our previous studies used AFM to investigate the physico-mechanical changes of nanoparticle-induced cell toxicity. Those results demonstrate the physico-mechanical changes as cellular response to treatment of nanomaterials, such as cell morphology, average cell height, RMS roughness and cell stiffness. This work ultimately highlights the interaction of biocompatible GQDs with cells and the consequent physico-mechanical changes, but it is not fatal to cells. We synthesized two types of GQDs – Glu-GQDs and Gr-GQDs – which have different functional groups on their surfaces. These GQDs that exhibit different fluorescence properties, but have a similar shape, produced a small amount of zROS and exhibited high biocompatibility. In addition, when changes in the biophysical and biomechanical properties were investigated, neither type of GQDs show a pernicious influence on the cell regardless of their functional groups. By analyzing the fluorescence emission, it could be understood that Glu-GQDs are distributed throughout the cells in both NHDF and HeLa cell lines; on the other hand, Gr-GQDs were distributed only in the cytoplasm of NHDF cells, while they were distributed throughout the cells in HeLa cells. In summary, we demonstrated that both GQDs could be potentially used for bio-imaging or as tracing agents in cells, regardless of the kinds of functional groups and the cellular response is quantified to establish a basic parameter in biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIP) (No. 2017R1A2B4004700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.12.055

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chaotic Dirac Billiard in graphene quantum dots
Science
Luminescent graphene quantum dots fabricated by pulsed laser synthesis
Carbon

Chem. Commun.
